planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination after natural cold stratification in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; plains larkspur is native to dry, open prairies and disturbed ground of the Great Plains and south-central US. Seeds require cold stratification to germinate well.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; plains larkspur is a native perennial of dry prairies and plains grasslands from Manitoba south to Texas and east to Illinois, producing pale white to pale blue flower spikes (more white or cream than the vivid blues of mountain larkspurs) on upright stems in late spring. The foliage and seeds are toxic to cattle and horses — a significant issue for rangeland management in the Great Plains — and all parts should be treated as toxic to humans as well. In the garden, the delicate, lacy foliage and tall flower spikes provide vertical interest in dry, native prairie plantings and meadow gardens. Self-sows in open, disturbed, dry conditions to naturalize over time. Important for pollinators including bumblebees, native bees, and hummingbirds during its spring bloom period.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall under oaks in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy or rocky, lean soil in partial shade; downy false foxglove is hemiparasitic on oak roots — it parasitically taps the roots of nearby oaks for water and nutrients while also producing chlorophyll. Plant near established oaks; it cannot survive without an oak host.

directions: Direct sow in fall near established oaks in slightly acidic, lean, sandy or rocky soil in partial shade; downy false foxglove is a hemiparasitic native perennial of the eastern US woodlands that grows in the company of oaks, tapping their root systems for supplemental water and nutrients while also photosynthesizing. The yellow, tubular, two-lipped flowers in late summer resemble yellow foxglove blossoms, providing nectar for bumblebees, hummingbirds, and clearwing sphinx moths at a time when most summer flowers are finished. The soft, downy, gray-green foliage is attractive. Will not grow without oak root host plants; plant near established black oak, red oak, or white oak for establishment. A fascinating and beautiful shade-garden plant that provides late-season color in oak-dominated woodland gardens. Self-sows under favorable conditions. Increasingly rare in some areas due to oak woodland fragmentation.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or direct sow in spring in moist to wet, very acidic, sandy or peaty, low-nutrient soil in full sun to partial shade; Virginia meadow beauty is adapted to the wet, acidic, nutrient-poor conditions of bogs, pitcher plant bogs, and moist pine savannas — it does not tolerate enriched garden soil or alkaline conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy or peaty, low-nutrient soil in full sun to partial shade; Virginia meadow beauty is a native perennial of wet, acidic bogs, pitcher plant bogs, moist pine savannas, and seepage-wet flatwoods. The four-petaled, bright rose-pink flowers with distinctive, curved, bright yellow anthers are produced from mid-summer through fall. The distinctive jug-shaped seed capsule that follows the flowers is attractive and ornamental, persisting into winter. Meadow beauties are poll-collected by specialist buzz pollinators — bumblebees and certain specialist bees that vibrate the anthers at a specific frequency to release pollen, making them important for native bee diversity. Requires very acidic, lean, moist soil; will not thrive in neutral or fertilized garden conditions. Perfect for bog gardens, pitcher plant gardens, and the wet, acidic margins of natural ponds.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty, low-nutrient soil in full sun to partial shade; Maryland meadow beauty requires moist, acidic, nutrient-poor, sandy conditions similar to its natural seepage bog and moist pine savanna habitat. Grows and spreads by rhizomes in appropriate wet, acidic areas.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty, low-nutrient soil in full sun to partial shade; Maryland meadow beauty is a widespread native of the eastern Coastal Plain, growing in wet roadside ditches, moist pine flatwoods, and seepage bogs from New England to Texas. The four-petaled pale rose-pink to white flowers with bright yellow, curved anthers are smaller and paler than Virginia meadow beauty, blooming from mid-summer through fall. Like all Rhexia species, it is pollinated primarily by buzz-pollinating bumblebees. The distinctive urn-shaped seed capsule with a distinctive neck ('the jug') is one of the most interesting wildflower fruits in the eastern flora. More adaptable to wet roadside and ditch conditions than many bog plants, making it useful for naturalistic plantings in moist, sandy, acidic areas. Spreads by rhizomes to form loose colonies. Essential for recreating the wet pine flatwood and sandhill bog plant community.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in very well-drained, dry, sandy or rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; desert phlox grows naturally on rocky, dry slopes, desert scrub edges, and chaparral margins of the Mojave Desert and surrounding mountains — highly drought-tolerant and adapted to low-rainfall desert conditions.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry, rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; desert phlox is a low, cushion-forming native phlox of rocky, dry slopes, desert scrub margins, and chaparral edges in the Mojave Desert and surrounding mountains of southern California, Nevada, and Utah. The plant forms compact, spreading mats of needle-like, stiff leaves that are characteristic of desert-adapted phlox. The flowers — white to pale lavender-pink — are profuse in early spring and provide an important early-season nectar source for native bees in desert ecosystems. Extremely drought and heat tolerant, requiring excellent drainage and minimal water; overwatering or clay soil causes rapid decline. An excellent native for dry, rocky, xeric rock gardens, desert-themed gardens, and the margins of gravel paths in arid Southwest gardens. Deer resistant and virtually pest-free in dry conditions.
